questions.text: When something good is happening in your life, you typically:

options.label: Feel grateful and enjoy it fully
Feel happy but slightly worried it won't last
Wait for the other shoe to drop
Find ways to undermine or complicate it

questions.text: How do you respond when someone compliments you?

options.label: Accept it graciously and say thank you
Reject it or point out why they're wrong

questions.text: When you're close to achieving a goal, you:

options.label: Push through with excitement and determination
Often give up or create obstacles

questions.text: What's your relationship with success?

options.label: I feel worthy of success and work toward it
I want success but sometimes doubt I deserve it
Success makes me uncomfortable or guilty
I fear success more than failure

questions.text: How do you handle positive attention?

options.label: I appreciate it but feel a bit self-conscious
I feel anxious and want to hide
I actively avoid or deflect it

questions.text: When you start succeeding at something new, you:

options.label: Feel proud and motivated to continue
Feel good but wonder if it's beginner's luck
Start questioning if you really deserve it
Find ways to quit or mess it up

questions.text: What happens when things are going 'too well'?

options.label: I appreciate it and stay present
I feel grateful but slightly anxious
I brace for something bad to happen
I unconsciously create problems or drama

questions.text: How comfortable are you with being happy?

options.label: Very uncomfortable - I distrust good feelings

questions.text: When someone treats you really well, you:

options.label: Feel grateful and treat them well in return
Appreciate it but wonder why they're so nice
Push them away or test them

questions.text: What's your relationship with receiving help?

options.label: I accept help graciously when needed
I accept but feel like I owe them
I'm uncomfortable and prefer to struggle alone
I refuse help even when I need it

questions.text: How do you respond to opportunities?

options.label: I evaluate and pursue exciting opportunities
I consider them carefully but sometimes overthink
I doubt whether I'm qualified or ready
I talk myself out of them or self-reject

questions.text: What's your inner dialogue about your worthiness?

options.label: I'm working on believing I'm worthy

questions.text: When you imagine your ideal future, you:

options.label: Feel doubtful it could happen for you

questions.text: How often do you procrastinate on important things?

options.label: Rarely - I handle important tasks promptly

questions.text: When stressed, you're most likely to:

options.label: Turn to avoidance or numbing behaviors

questions.text: How do you handle conflict in relationships?

options.label: Try to resolve it but sometimes avoid
Avoid it or blow it out of proportion
Create bigger conflict or sabotage the relationship

questions.text: When you commit to something, you:

options.label: Usually follow through with occasional slip-ups
Start strong but often don't finish

questions.text: How do you treat yourself when you make a mistake?

options.label: With some self-criticism but overall kindness

questions.text: What's your pattern with healthy habits?

options.label: I maintain them but occasionally slip

questions.text: How do you respond to your own boundaries?

options.label: I set them but sometimes compromise
I set them but regularly violate them
I don't set boundaries for myself

questions.text: When you're doing well, your habits:

options.label: Stay consistent - they support my success
Start to slip - I relax too much

questions.text: How do you handle success or progress?

options.label: Build on it and keep momentum

questions.text: What's your relationship with self-care?

options.label: It's a priority I maintain consistently
I practice it but not always consistently
I know I should but rarely do it

questions.text: How often do you keep promises to yourself?
When things get hard, you:
What's your most common self-talk?
How do you view your past failures?

options.label: As learning experiences and growth opportunities

questions.text: What do you believe you deserve?

options.label: Good things but I have to earn them
Only what I can achieve through struggle
Not much - good things happen to others

questions.text: How do you explain your successes to yourself?

options.label: I earned them through effort and ability
Combination of my work and good timing

questions.text: What's your core belief about yourself?
When you look in the mirror, your first thought is:
How do you complete this: 'People like me...'
What's your relationship with your potential?

options.label: I'm actively working to reach it
I believe in it but face obstacles
I doubt I have much potential
I don't believe I have meaningful potential

questions.text: How do you view your worthiness of love?

options.label: I'm worthy of love as I am
I'm worthy but need to work on myself
I'll be worthy when I'm better/different
I'm fundamentally unworthy of real love

questions.text: When you achieve something, your inner voice says:

options.label: 'I did it! I'm proud of myself'
'That's good, but I could have done better'

questions.text: What do you believe about change?

options.label: Real change is unlikely for me
I can't really change who I am

questions.text: How do you view your mistakes?

options.label: Natural part of learning and growth

questions.text: What's your belief about happiness?
How aware are you of your self-sabotaging patterns?

options.label: Very aware - I actively work on them
Not aware - I don't see the patterns

questions.text: When you notice yourself self-sabotaging, you:

options.label: Stop and redirect to healthier choices
Notice but don't always stop it
Notice but feel powerless to stop
Don't notice until after the damage

questions.text: How often do you repeat the same self-destructive patterns?
What triggers your self-sabotage most?

options.label: I've identified and manage my triggers

questions.text: How do your relationships typically end?

options.label: Me pushing them away or creating distance
Me sabotaging when things get good

questions.text: What's your pattern with opportunities?

options.label: I sometimes hesitate but usually go for it
I actively avoid or ruin them

questions.text: How do you handle periods of stability?

options.label: I enjoy them but worry a bit

questions.text: What happens when you're close to a breakthrough?

options.label: I push through to achieve it
I feel resistance but work through it
I often quit right before succeeding
I always sabotage right at the finish line

questions.text: How much do you repeat your family's patterns?

options.label: I'm living out the same patterns

questions.text: What's your relationship with therapy or self-work?

options.label: I know I should but resist
I avoid it or sabotage the process

questions.text: When you identify a pattern, you:

options.label: Create a plan to change it

questions.text: How ready are you to change your self-sabotaging patterns?

options.label: Very ready - actively working on change
Not ready - patterns feel safer than change

archetypes.name: The Self-Aware Overcomer
archetypes.description: You have strong awareness of any self-limiting patterns and actively work to overcome them. You practice self-compassion, maintain healthy habits, and believe in your capacity for growth and change.
suggestions: Continue your self-work and celebrate progress
Share your growth practices with others
Stay vigilant during stressful periods
Remember that maintaining growth is also growth

archetypes.name: The Imposter
archetypes.description: Despite evidence of your capabilities, you doubt your worthiness and attribute success to luck or external factors. You fear being 'found out' and unconsciously undermine your achievements to match your internal self-image.
characteristics: Fear of being exposed as fraud
suggestions: Keep a success journal documenting your contributions
Challenge thoughts about luck with evidence
Practice accepting compliments without deflecting
Work with a therapist on core beliefs about worthiness
Connect with others who experience imposter syndrome

archetypes.name: The Upper Limit Problem
archetypes.description: You unconsciously have a 'set point' for how much success, love, or happiness you can tolerate. When you exceed it, you self-sabotage back to your comfort zone. Good things make you anxious because they feel unfamiliar.
characteristics: Has a tolerance ceiling for good feelings
suggestions: Read 'The Big Leap' by Gay Hendricks
Practice tolerating good feelings in small doses
Notice when you're about to self-sabotage and pause
Work on expanding your capacity for happiness
Challenge beliefs about deserving good things

archetypes.name: The Perfectionist Procrastinator
archetypes.description: Your impossibly high standards paralyze you. You procrastinate because you fear failure, so you protect your ego by not really trying. 'I didn't fail, I just didn't do my best' becomes your shield against vulnerability.
characteristics: Uses lack of effort as excuse
suggestions: Practice 'good enough' rather than perfect
Set process goals instead of outcome goals
Take small imperfect actions daily
Work on separating self-worth from performance
Challenge perfectionist thoughts with evidence

archetypes.name: The Repeater
archetypes.description: You repeat the same painful patterns across relationships, jobs, and life situations. You might be unconsciously recreating family dynamics or trying to master old wounds. The patterns feel familiar, even when they hurt.
characteristics: Repeats same patterns across life areas
suggestions: Work with a therapist on family-of-origin issues
Journal about patterns across different life areas
Pause before making major decisions - is this the pattern?
Build new neural pathways through different choices
Surround yourself with people who model healthier patterns

archetypes.name: The Self-Punisher
archetypes.description: You carry deep shame or guilt and punish yourself through self-sabotage. You don't believe you deserve good things, so you unconsciously ensure you don't have them. Your inner critic is brutal and constant.
suggestions: Seek therapy - this requires professional support
Practice self-compassion exercises daily
Work on trauma healing (EMDR, IFS, somatic work)
Challenge beliefs about deserving punishment
Build relationship with inner child work
Consider whether religious or family shame needs processing
